prompt: Complete: 'Il ragazzo ____ parla è mio fratello.'
explanation: 'Il ragazzo che parla è mio fratello' means 'The boy who is speaking is my brother.' To hang a description on a noun, Italian uses a joining word, the relative pronoun. Here the relative stands for the subject of its own clause (the boy does the speaking), and for a plain subject with no preposition Italian reaches for che. Che never changes form: il ragazzo che, la ragazza che, i ragazzi che.
examiner_note: Common miss per bucket attributes: il quale where plain che suffices; the must_not_include catches it as attempted-but-wrong. chi is ungrammatical with an antecedent and is also caught.

prompt: Complete: 'Ho un'amica ____ abita a Napoli.'
explanation: 'Ho un'amica che abita a Napoli' means 'I have a friend who lives in Naples.' The relative pronoun che links the description back to amica, and since the friend is the one doing the living (the subject of the clause), plain che is the word. No agreement is needed: che stays the same for masculine, feminine, singular and plural.

prompt: Complete: 'Non mi piacciono le persone ____ arrivano sempre in ritardo.'
explanation: 'Non mi piacciono le persone che arrivano sempre in ritardo' means 'I don't like people who always arrive late.' The relative pronoun che stands for the subject of arrivano. English switches between who and which; Italian che covers both, and it does not pluralise either: le persone che, exactly like la persona che.
examiner_note: le quali is grammatical but stilted here; caught as the over-formal miss. chi cannot follow an antecedent.

prompt: Complete: 'Il treno ____ parte alle otto è già pieno.'
explanation: 'Il treno che parte alle otto è già pieno' means 'The train that leaves at eight is already full.' The relative pronoun che introduces the clause describing the train, and the train is the subject of parte, so plain che does the job. Che works for people and things alike; there is no who/which split to manage.

prompt: Complete: 'Il libro ____ leggo è bello.'
explanation: 'Il libro che leggo è bello' means 'The book (that) I'm reading is nice.' This time the relative pronoun stands for the thing being read, the direct object of leggo, and Italian still uses plain che. English often drops the word ('the book I'm reading'); Italian never drops it. And there is no preposition here, so cui has no business in the sentence.
examiner_note: Common miss per bucket attributes: cui for a direct object; caught.

prompt: Complete: 'La pizza ____ hai fatto era buonissima.'
explanation: 'La pizza che hai fatto era buonissima' means 'The pizza you made was delicious.' The pizza is what got made, the direct object of hai fatto, and a direct object with no preposition takes plain che. Notice English can say 'the pizza you made' with nothing at all; Italian must say che.

prompt: Complete: 'Le scarpe ____ ho comprato ieri sono strette.'

markpoints.label: che as direct object, plural antecedent

explanation: 'Le scarpe che ho comprato ieri sono strette' means 'The shoes I bought yesterday are tight.' The shoes are what I bought, so the relative pronoun is the direct object of its clause, and that means invariable che, plural antecedent or not. Le scarpe che, exactly like la scarpa che.

prompt: Complete: 'L'uomo ____ abbiamo incontrato al mercato è il sindaco.'

markpoints.label: che as direct object, person antecedent

explanation: 'L'uomo che abbiamo incontrato al mercato è il sindaco' means 'The man we met at the market is the mayor.' The man is the one being met, the direct object of abbiamo incontrato. A direct object relative is che even for a person; English 'whom' tempts learners toward something fancier, but Italian keeps plain che.

prompt: Complete: 'Il film ____ parlavo è ancora al cinema.'
explanation: 'Il film di cui parlavo è ancora al cinema' means 'The film I was talking about is still on at the cinema.' Parlare takes di ('parlare di qualcosa'), and when a relative pronoun carries a preposition, che steps aside for cui, with the preposition sitting in front: di cui. English lets the preposition dangle at the end ('the film I was talking about'); Italian moves it up front and never strands it. The agreeing alternative del quale is also correct and a touch more formal.
examiner_note: Bare cui without a preposition is literary shorthand for 'a cui' only; here it would be wrong (the verb needs di), so it sits in must_not_include. Engine order (any_phrases first) means correct 'di cui' answers are never caught by the bare-cui guard.

prompt: Complete: 'È un collega ____ lavoro da dieci anni.'
explanation: 'È un collega con cui lavoro da dieci anni' means 'He's a colleague I've worked with for ten years.' Working WITH someone is lavorare con, and a relative that carries a preposition switches from che to cui, preposition in front: con cui. The killer error is copying English word order and stranding the preposition ('un collega che lavoro con'); Italian never leaves the preposition behind.
examiner_note: The stranded-preposition attempt contains che and is caught by must_not_include.

prompt: Complete: 'La ragazza ____ ho dato il mio numero non mi ha chiamato.'
explanation: 'La ragazza a cui ho dato il mio numero non mi ha chiamato' means 'The girl I gave my number to never called me.' You give something TO someone (dare a), so the relative needs the preposition, and preposition + relative means a cui, never a che. The formal agreeing alternative alla quale also works.
examiner_note: Two engine caveats: (1) literary bare 'cui' (= a cui) is not credited at this level, and by check order it falls through to a silent miss rather than a wrong-flag; (2) a wrong 'da cui' attempt contains the substring 'a cui' and would be falsely credited - known substring-engine limitation, flagged in coverage notes.

prompt: Complete: 'Il motivo ____ sono partito è un segreto.'
explanation: 'Il motivo per cui sono partito è un segreto' means 'The reason (why) I left is a secret.' Italian says 'the reason FOR which': the preposition per attaches to the relative, so che gives way to per cui. This is also how Italian renders English 'why' after 'reason'; perché belongs to questions, not here.

prompt: Complete: 'Non mi piace il modo ____ mi parli.'
explanation: 'Non mi piace il modo in cui mi parli' means 'I don't like the way you talk to me.' The way you do something is the manner IN which you do it: in cui. English hides the preposition completely ('the way you talk to me'), which is exactly why this one is worth drilling; the Italian relative cannot go without it. Dove is only for places, so it cannot rescue the sentence either.
examiner_note: Non-place in-cui context chosen deliberately so that dove is a catchable wrong answer rather than a valid alternative.

prompt: Complete: 'È una persona ____ puoi sempre contare.'
explanation: 'È una persona su cui puoi sempre contare' means 'She's someone you can always count on.' Contare takes su ('contare su qualcuno'), and the preposition travels to the front of the relative: su cui. English strands it ('count on'); Italian fronts it. The verb chooses the preposition, so learning verb + preposition pairs is half of mastering cui.
examiner_note: a cui is wrong here (contare governs su); included in must_not_include as the wrong-preposition diagnostic.

prompt: Complete: 'Lo scrittore ____ libro ho letto vive a Torino.'
explanation: 'Lo scrittore il cui libro ho letto vive a Torino' means 'The writer whose book I read lives in Turin.' For 'whose', Italian slips cui between an article and the noun: il cui libro. The article agrees with the thing possessed, not with the owner; libro is masculine singular, so il cui, regardless of who the writer is. Di cui does not express possession here.
examiner_note: Common miss per bucket attributes: di cui for possession, and wrong article agreement; both in must_not_include.

prompt: Complete: 'Il professore ____ figlia studia con me è molto famoso.'

markpoints.label: la cui, f sg possessed, owner/possessed clash

explanation: 'Il professore la cui figlia studia con me è molto famoso' means 'The professor whose daughter studies with me is very famous.' The 'whose' pattern is article + cui + noun, and the article looks at the possessed noun: figlia is feminine singular, so la cui, even though the professor (the owner) is masculine. Matching the article to the owner is the classic trap.
examiner_note: Owner masculine, possessed feminine: the article-agreement clash is the diagnostic. di cui for possession also caught.

prompt: Complete: 'Il poeta ____ opere studiamo è del Trecento.'
explanation: 'Il poeta le cui opere studiamo è del Trecento' means 'The poet whose works we study is from the fourteenth century.' Article + cui + noun renders 'whose', and the article agrees with the possessed noun: opere is feminine plural, so le cui. The poet's own gender is irrelevant to the choice.
examiner_note: opere entry points at the canonical lemma opera; surface form listed so the word is clickable.

prompt: Complete: 'L'attrice ____ ultimo film ha vinto tre premi è qui stasera.'

markpoints.label: il cui, m sg possessed, owner/possessed clash

explanation: 'L'attrice il cui ultimo film ha vinto tre premi è qui stasera' means 'The actress whose latest film won three prizes is here tonight.' The article in the 'whose' construction agrees with the possessed noun: film is masculine singular, so il cui, even though the owner, l'attrice, is feminine. The feminine pull of attrice toward la cui is exactly the miss this item hunts.
examiner_note: Sharpest safe diagnostic in the leaf: feminine owner, masculine singular possessed. Masculine PLURAL possessed (i cui) cannot be marked safely: 'i cui' is a substring of 'di cui', so the commonest miss would be falsely credited. i cui coverage moved to the translation strand; flagged in coverage notes.

prompt: Complete: 'La sorella di Marco, ____ abita a Roma, arriva domani.' (make it unambiguous that it is the sister who lives in Rome)

markpoints.label: la quale to disambiguate, f sg

explanation: 'La sorella di Marco, la quale abita a Roma, arriva domani' means 'Marco's sister, who lives in Rome, is arriving tomorrow.' Plain che would leave the reader guessing whether the sister or Marco lives in Rome. The agreeing relative il quale carries gender and number, so la quale can only point at the sister. Reaching for the agreeing form exactly when che would be ambiguous is the skill here.
examiner_note: che is grammatical but fails the disambiguation the prompt asks for; caught as the ambiguity miss.

prompt: Complete: 'Il marito di Anna, ____ lavora in banca, è molto simpatico.' (it is the husband who works in the bank)

markpoints.label: il quale to disambiguate, m sg

explanation: 'Il marito di Anna, il quale lavora in banca, è molto simpatico' means 'Anna's husband, who works in the bank, is very nice.' With two possible antecedents (the husband, Anna), che would leave 'who works in the bank' floating between them. The agreeing relative settles it: masculine singular il quale can only be the husband. Agreement is doing real work, not decoration.

prompt: Complete: 'Ho conosciuto la figlia del professore, ____ studia medicina.' (the daughter studies medicine)

markpoints.label: la quale to disambiguate, f sg

explanation: 'Ho conosciuto la figlia del professore, la quale studia medicina' means 'I met the professor's daughter, who studies medicine.' After 'la figlia del professore', che could describe either person. The agreeing relative la quale locks onto the feminine noun, the daughter, and the ambiguity dissolves. This is the one context where the heavyweight il quale beats everyday che.

prompt: Complete: 'L'azienda ____ lavoro produce mobili. (per + quale)'
explanation: 'L'azienda per la quale lavoro produce mobili' means 'The company I work for makes furniture.' After a preposition the agreeing relative il quale is a full alternative to cui, but unlike cui it must agree: azienda is feminine singular, so per la quale. Article and quale move together as a unit.
examiner_note: per cui is equally correct Italian but the (per + quale) cue asks for the agreeing family so agreement gets tested; a per cui answer is a cue-miss and deliberately not in must_not_include.

prompt: Complete: 'Gli amici ____ sono andato in vacanza tornano oggi. (con + quale)'
explanation: 'Gli amici con i quali sono andato in vacanza tornano oggi' means 'The friends I went on holiday with are coming back today.' The agreeing relative after a preposition must match its antecedent: amici is masculine plural, so con i quali. The singular con il quale is the classic slip when the plural antecedent sits a few words back.

prompt: Complete: 'La conferenza ____ ho assistito era molto noiosa. (a + quale)'

markpoints.label: alla quale, articulated a + f sg

explanation: 'La conferenza alla quale ho assistito era molto noiosa' means 'The lecture I attended was very boring.' Assistere takes a, and with il quale the preposition fuses into an articulated form: a + la quale gives alla quale. Two agreements ride on one word: gender and number from conferenza, and the correct fusion of preposition and article.
examiner_note: Substring caveat: a wrong 'dalla quale' attempt contains 'alla quale' and would be falsely credited by the engine; low likelihood given the (a + quale) cue, flagged in coverage notes.

prompt: Complete: 'I colleghi ____ parlavo sono appena usciti. (di + quale)'

markpoints.label: dei quali, articulated di + m pl

explanation: 'I colleghi dei quali parlavo sono appena usciti' means 'The colleagues I was talking about have just left.' Parlare di plus the agreeing relative: di + i quali fuses to dei quali, masculine plural to match colleghi. The agreeing family always drags its article along, and after a preposition that article contracts exactly as it would before any noun.
examiner_note: di cui equally correct; excluded from must_not_include because the (di + quale) cue makes it a cue-miss, not the diagnostic.

prompt: Complete: '____ studia ogni giorno impara in fretta.'
explanation: 'Chi studia ogni giorno impara in fretta' means 'Whoever studies every day learns quickly.' When there is no noun to lean on, no 'the person' spelled out, Italian uses the antecedent-less relative chi: it means 'the one who / whoever' all by itself. Che always needs a noun in front of it; chi never has one.
examiner_note: quello che for a person is marginal here; bare che is flatly wrong. The che guard catches both (substring).

prompt: Complete: '____ dorme non piglia pesci.'
explanation: 'Chi dorme non piglia pesci' is the proverb 'Whoever sleeps catches no fish' (the early bird catches the worm). Proverbs love the antecedent-less relative chi because it names nobody in particular: 'the one who...'. No noun before it, singular verb after it; that is chi's fixed shape.

prompt: Complete: 'Chi ____ può restare ancora un po'. (volere)'
explanation: 'Chi vuole può restare ancora un po'' means 'Whoever wants to can stay a little longer.' Even when chi points at a whole roomful of people, it is grammatically singular and its verb stays third-person singular: vuole, never vogliono. English 'those who want' pulls hard toward the plural; resist it.
examiner_note: Verb chosen so the singular is not a substring of the plural (vuole/vogliono); arrivare was rejected because 'arriva' sits inside 'arrivano' and the engine's positive match would credit the plural miss.

prompt: Complete: 'Chi ____ queste cose non capisce niente. (dire)'

markpoints.label: chi takes singular verb, irregular dire

explanation: 'Chi dice queste cose non capisce niente' means 'Anyone who says these things understands nothing.' Chi is always third-person singular, so dire gives dice. The meaning is naturally plural, 'people who say this', which is precisely why the plural dicono keeps tempting learners; chi refuses it.

prompt: Complete: 'Mi piace ____ hai fatto.'

infinitive.reveal: piacere - to please (mi piace - I like)

explanation: 'Mi piace quello che hai fatto' means 'I like what you did.' English 'what' smuggles in a hidden phrase, 'the thing that', and Italian spells the whole thing out: quello che (or its more formal twin ciò che, or clipped quel che). A bare che cannot mean 'what'; it needs quello in front to supply the missing 'thing'.
examiner_note: cosa would build an indirect question, ungrammatical after piacere; the matrix verb was chosen so cosa is a catchable wrong answer rather than a valid alternative.

prompt: Complete: '____ mi hai detto ieri è vero.'

markpoints.label: neuter relative as subject of the sentence

explanation: 'Quello che mi hai detto ieri è vero' means 'What you told me yesterday is true.' The whole chunk 'what you told me' acts as the subject of è, and Italian builds it as quello che (or ciò che): the quello supplies the 'thing', the che hooks the clause onto it. Starting the sentence with bare che or with cosa leaves the 'thing' unexpressed and the sentence broken.

prompt: Complete: 'Ho capito tutto ____ hai spiegato.'
explanation: 'Ho capito tutto quello che hai spiegato' means 'I understood everything you explained.' After tutto, English says just 'everything you explained', so the temptation is 'tutto che', but Italian insists on the full neuter relative: tutto quello che or tutto ciò che. Tutto che does not exist.
examiner_note: The bare-che trap ('tutto che') is the headline miss here; caught by must_not_include.

prompt: Complete: '____ conta davvero è la salute.'

markpoints.label: ciò che / quello che as formal subject

explanation: 'Ciò che conta davvero è la salute' means 'What really matters is health.' Sentences of the shape 'What matters is...' open with the neuter relative: ciò che in writing and careful speech, quello che everywhere. Chi would make it 'the person who counts', a different sentence; bare che makes no sentence at all.

prompt: Complete: 'La città ____ vivo è piccola.'
explanation: 'La città dove vivo è piccola' means 'The city where I live is small.' For places, Italian has a relative 'where': dove simply follows the place noun, exactly like English. The bookish alternatives in cui and nella quale say the same thing ('in which'). What never works is bare che: a place relative needs the 'in' idea expressed, and che cannot carry it.
examiner_note: dove, in cui and nella quale all credited at 1.0; the bucket's common_miss (che for a place) is the guard.

prompt: Complete: 'Il ristorante ____ abbiamo cenato ieri era carissimo.'
explanation: 'Il ristorante dove abbiamo cenato ieri era carissimo' means 'The restaurant where we had dinner yesterday was really expensive.' A place noun followed by 'where something happened' takes the relative dove (or in cui / nel quale). English speakers rarely miss this one from English, but the che habit from other relatives spills over; che alone cannot mean 'where'.

prompt: Complete: 'Questo è il paese ____ sono nati i miei nonni.'
explanation: 'Questo è il paese dove sono nati i miei nonni' means 'This is the village where my grandparents were born.' Place antecedent, so the relative is dove, with in cui and nel quale as the formal equivalents. Note the verb agrees with i nonni, which follows it; the relative itself stays untouched by any agreement, which is dove's charm.

prompt: Complete: 'Il film ____ abbiamo visto ieri era noioso.' Choose: che / a cui.

markpoints.label: che chosen: direct object, no preposition

explanation: 'Il film che abbiamo visto ieri era noioso' means 'The film we saw yesterday was boring.' The test is the relative's job in its own clause: the film is what we saw, a plain direct object, and vedere brings no preposition. No preposition means che; cui only ever appears with a preposition in front (a cui, di cui, con cui). Ask 'does the verb need a preposition with this noun?' and the choice makes itself.

prompt: Complete: 'L'amico ____ ho prestato la macchina è partito per il mare.' Choose: che / a cui.
explanation: 'L'amico a cui ho prestato la macchina è partito per il mare' means 'The friend I lent the car to has left for the seaside.' You lend something TO someone: prestare a. The relative has to carry that preposition, and a preposition rules out che; it must be a cui. English hides the clue by stranding 'to' at the end of the sentence, which is why this choice needs drilling.

prompt: Complete: 'La squadra ____ tifiamo ha vinto il campionato.' Choose: che / per cui.
explanation: 'La squadra per cui tifiamo ha vinto il campionato' means 'The team we support won the league.' Tifare takes per ('tifare per una squadra'), so the relative must carry per, and that means per cui, not che. English 'the team we support' shows no preposition at all, so the choice hinges on knowing the Italian verb's own preposition, which is exactly what the item tests.

prompt: Complete: 'La moglie del direttore, ____ arriva sempre presto, ci saluta ogni mattina.' Choose: che / la quale. (it is the wife who arrives early)
explanation: 'La moglie del direttore, la quale arriva sempre presto, ci saluta ogni mattina' means 'The director's wife, who always arrives early, greets us every morning.' With two nouns in play, che would leave 'who arrives early' dangling between wife and director. The agreeing relative carries gender: la quale is feminine, so it can only be the wife. When che is ambiguous, il quale earns its keep; that is the whole choice.

prompt: Complete: 'Il ragazzo ____ gioca in porta è mio cugino.' Choose: che / il quale.

markpoints.label: che chosen: no ambiguity, plain che is natural

explanation: 'Il ragazzo che gioca in porta è mio cugino' means 'The boy who plays in goal is my cousin.' Only one person is in view, so nothing needs disambiguating, and everyday Italian takes plain che. Il quale is not ungrammatical here, it is just stiff: reserve the agreeing relative for when che would be ambiguous or after a preposition. Reaching for il quale everywhere is the over-correction this item catches.
examiner_note: il quale is a stylistic rather than grammatical miss; the explanation says so explicitly. Kept per dispatch: do not push learners toward il quale where plain che is natural.
